section: A modified Johnson-Cook model to determine plastic flow behavior of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8 C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the paper on Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed by multiaxial forging (MAF) up to equivalent strain 2.3. The abstract reports key findings: 0,1,3,5 passes with 33% normal strain per pass; XRD and EBSD characterization; dislocation density increase from 0.79e15 to 8.98e15 m^-2; microstrain rise from 3.66e-3 to 6.8e-3; grain refinement from 50 μm to 13 μm; yield strength from 380 to 1528 MPa, UTS from 762 to 1548 MPa. Strengthening mechanisms are discussed, with dislocation strengthening dominant up to 3 passes. A modified Johnson-Cook model with dislocation density and grain size is proposed and implemented via ABAQUS VUMAT to predict flow stress and yield strength per pass. The introduction surveys MAF literature: temperature and strain rate effects, strain distribution uniformity, and prior data on austenitic steels, SUS316SS, and interstitial-free steel. Figures show MAF schematic (Fig. 1) and forged billets/tensile samples (Fig. 2). This chunk sets the stage for subsequent experimental details, microstructural analysis, constitutive model formulation, and FE simulations.
processing_route: temperature: warm (specific value not yet stated)
properties: ductility >70% at RT and >95% at 300°C (from literature)
mechanisms: dislocation strengthening significant up to 3 passes
figures_or_tables: Fig. 2: forged billets and cut tensile samples
unresolved_terms: modified J-C model parameters and calibration
FE simulation setup and validation details

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the experimental methodology and initial definitions for the study of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed by multiaxial forging (MAF). The material was vacuum induction melted, homogenized (1200°C/2h), hot forged (1200–900°C, >60%), solution treated (1050°C/1h, WQ), resulting in actual composition Fe-30.6Mn-9.1Al-0.79C and density 6.8 g/cm³. MAF billets (30×20×20 mm) were lubricated with MoS2, heated to 250°C, and deformed at 1 mm/s. Each pass imparts 33% normal strain (equivalent strain 0.47); samples up to 5 passes were studied. Table 1 provides XRD-derived crystallite size (61 nm to 10.12 nm), microstrain (3.66×10⁻³ to 6.88×10⁻³), and dislocation density (0.79×10¹⁵ to 8.98×10¹⁵ m⁻²) for 0,1,3,5 passes. EBSD employed >15° grain boundaries for grain size. No carbide evolution was seen by XRD (Fig. 5). The modified J-C model incorporates grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch, k=2374 MPa) and dislocation strengthening (Taylor, α=0.24, M=3.0, G=61 GPa). FE simulation in ABAQUS/Explicit uses 41382 C3D8R elements (0.6 mm) and friction 0.15. Tensile tests were defined (RT, 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹) but results are not yet reported. Subsequent chunks should build on these processing-microstructure links, the quantified XRD data, and the model parameters to evaluate predicted flow stress and validate the FE model.
processing_route: Vacuum induction melting, 600 mbar Ar during Mn addition
MAF: 30×20×20 mm billets, MoS2 lubrication, 250°C preheat, 1 mm/s, 33% normal strain per pass, equivalent strain 0.47/pass, max 5 passes
key_variables: Taylor parameters: α=0.24, M=3.0, G=61 GPa
microstructure: Dislocation density via Eq. (1) from crystallite size and microstrain
Table 1: all XRD results per pass
properties: Tensile testing configured (RT, 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹, 2 specimens/pass) – results pending in later chunks
mechanisms: Modified J-C model merges both into flow stress
figures_or_tables: Fig. 3(a,b): sampling positions and tensile specimen dimensions
Fig. 4: half-sectional FE model before/after compression
Fig. 5: XRD patterns showing no carbide evolution
Fig. 6: dislocation density vs. equivalent strain (measured + predicted)
Table 1: XRD crystallite size, microstrain, dislocation density

context_update.summary: This chunk reports XRD and EBSD microstructural characterization of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250 °C up to 5 passes (equivalent strain 2.3). XRD confirmed a fully austenitic structure and quantified dislocation density increasing from 0.79×10^15 m⁻² (MAF-0) to 8.98×10^15 m⁻² (MAF-5), which was fitted to an exponential saturation model (Eq. 7) with k1=7.9×10^15 m⁻² and k2=1.813; deceleration and possible dislocation rearrangement were observed at critical strain 2.3. EBSD image quality maps revealed high-angle boundary fraction initially decreased from 0.62 to 0.24 (MAF-3) and then increased to 0.69 (MAF-5) due to conversion of low-angle boundaries. Texture evolved from strong [101] alignment in MAF-0 to random orientations after multiple passes. Grain size refined from 50 μm to 13 μm, following an exponential decay (k3=0.639, R²=0.99052). KAM maps confirmed increasing plastic strain from 0.34° to 1.07°. These microstructural metrics provide the experimental foundation for the modified Johnson-Cook model and upcoming FE validation. Subsequent chunks should incorporate these data into flow stress predictions and report tensile properties.
microstructure: grain size: MAF-0 50 μm, MAF-1 35 μm, MAF-3 20 μm, MAF-5 13 μm
texture: strong [101] and [111] // ND in MAF-0, randomized with passes

context_update.summary: The current chunk reports the mechanical properties of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250 °C for 0, 1, 3, and 5 passes. Yield strength rises from 380 to 1528 MPa, ultimate tensile strength from 762 to 1548 MPa, while total elongation decreases from 75% to 8% (uniform elongation 63% to 0.8%). Work hardening capability declines with passes. Strengthening is attributed to dislocation density increase (from 0.79 to 8.98×10^15 m⁻²) and grain refinement (50 to 13 μm); dislocation strengthening dominates early stages (1-3 passes), while grain boundary strengthening dominates later (3-5 passes), as quantified in Table 2. No deformation twinning; negligible annealing twin fractions (0.007 in MAF-1, 0.003 in MAF-5). The section then describes finite element modeling using a modified Johnson-Cook model implemented via ABAQUS VUMAT with six state variables (Table 3) and material properties (Table 4). Simulation maps (Fig. 12) show strain concentration in the center, with maximum strain ~0.6 after 1 pass and ~2.6 after 5 passes. Predicted yield strengths (Fig. 13) match experimental values well up to 3 passes but are lower after 5 passes, possibly due to unaccounted texture effects. Subsequent chunks should note the FE model’s limitations and may address texture influence.
mechanisms: grain boundary strengthening dominant later (3-5 passes)
work hardening ability decreases with increasing passes due to high dislocation density and microstrain
texture randomization may increase isotropic strength, causing simulation to underestimate yield
strain concentrates in central region leading to higher local deformation

(1) from crystallite size and microstrain; EBSD: grain size from >15° boundaries; Table 1: all XRD results per pass
Properties: yield strength 380 to 1528 MPa; UTS 762 to 1548 MPa; ductility >70% at RT and >95% at 300°C (from literature); Tensile testing configured (RT, 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹, 2 specimens/pass) – results pending in later chunks; YS: 380, 1040, 1279, 1528 MPa; UTS: 762, 1368, 1415, 1548 MPa; Total elongation: 75%, 30%, 13.7%, 8%; Uniform elongation: 63%, -, 3%, 0.8%
Mechanisms: dislocation strengthening significant up to 3 passes; grain boundary strengthening; strain hardening; Grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch, k=2374 MPa); Dislocation strengthening (Taylor equation); Modified J-C model merges both into flow stress; dislocation accumulation and rearrangement; grain refinement

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Section 5.4 (Finite element analysis of MAF) and the Conclusions. The FE analysis confirms that the modified Johnson-Cook model agrees well with experimental dislocation density, grain size, and yield strength up to 3 passes, but after 3 passes the simulated yield strength deviates lower than experiments, partly attributed to unaccounted texture effects. The simulated yield strength map (Fig. 14) shows maximum strength at the center decreasing toward the surface. The investigation concludes that MAF of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C steel at 250°C raises tensile strength from 762 to 1548 MPa while ductility drops from 75% to 8%. Strengthening is dominated by dislocation density in early passes (1–3) and by grain boundary strengthening in later passes (3–5). The maximum equivalent strain reached is 2.3. The modified J-C model is validated for cold deformation processes. Key figures are Fig. 13 (yield strength comparison), Fig. 14 (yield strength map), Fig. 5 (XRD dislocation density), and Fig. 8 (EBSD grain size). No further data chunks are expected; future chunks may refer to these conclusions.
mechanisms: Dislocation strengthening dominant up to 3 passes
Grain boundary strengthening dominant from 3 to 5 passes
Texture effects not included in simulation, causing underprediction of strength
